Q1: I tried to keep things organized by blocking out study time in my calendar, usually around 4-5 hours most days. Being a visual learner, I'd make color-coded schedules and stick them on my wall. With 20 online courses to manage, I had to be pretty systematic or I'd fall behind. I'd usually group similar subjects together and tackle them in themed study sessions, which helped me stay focused despite everything feeling a bit overwhelming sometimes.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular because I knew if I let things slide, I'd never catch up with so many courses. I tried to study at the same times each day, usually mornings when my mind was clearer. But honestly, when deadlines piled up, everything went out the window and I'd just focus on whatever was due soonest. The stress of that unpredictability scared me, so I really tried to stick to my routine as much as possible.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd check just to make sure I hadn't missed anything important because the fear of falling behind was always there. The gamified tasks actually helped prompt me to log in more regularly since I could see my progress and it felt less intimidating than just staring at a list of assignments.
Q4: It took a few weeks, but eventually checking the LMS became part of my morning routine along with coffee. The gamified elements made it feel less like a chore and more like checking off achievements. I'd log in, see what badges or points I could earn, and that gave me a small sense of accomplishment even when motivation was low. It wasn't exciting, but it became automatic.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch confusing parts. I also really appreciated when lecturers used diagrams or flowcharts. The gamified task system helped too because it broke big assignments into smaller, less scary pieces. Discussion boards were okay when people actually responded thoughtfully, which helped me see different perspectives.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading PDFs because with limited resources, I couldn't always print them and reading long documents on screen hurt my eyes. Some of the optional supplementary materials felt like too much when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later, but later never really came because there was always something more urgent.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to my grades or when it helped something finally click in my understanding. The gamified tasks were good at this because earning points felt like tangible progress. If something seemed like busywork or I couldn't see how it would help me pass, I'd deprioritize it even if I knew deep down it might be useful.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on when required work was piling up. There was this constant guilt about not doing them, but also relief that they weren't mandatory. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, which honestly was the main thing that got me to do them. The optional stuff just didn't create enough urgency to compete with everything else.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up online. I'd download lecture slides and diagrams, then study from printed copies or hand-drawn notes because that's how I learn best visually. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. Sometimes I'd study with friends from my extracurriculars who were taking similar courses, and we'd work through problems together.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or creating visual mind maps that the LMS would never capture. I'd often watch a video once online, screenshot the important diagrams, and then study those images repeatedly offline. Group study sessions at the library with friends were really valuable but completely invisible to the system. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for a lot of content. I'd access a lecture or resource once, download everything I could, and then work with it offline for days. With my limited internet situation at home sometimes, it was just more practical. I'd annotate PDFs on my tablet or print things out when I could afford to, and that's where the real studying happened.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. I had multiple deadlines converging, my stress levels were through the roof, and I felt scared that I was going to fail despite all my effort. There were days when I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, and just feel paralyzed. The gamified tasks helped a little because at least I could do small things and feel like I was making progress.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working part-time to help with finances, the LMS became something I'd just skim through rather than deeply engage with. Confusion about course requirements made me avoid certain modules entirely until I absolutely had to face them. My extracurriculars also competed for time, and sometimes I'd choose those because they felt less stressful than coursework.
Q14: There were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about money or other responsibilities. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what anyone was saying. Sometimes I'd open readings and stare at the words but nothing would sink in because I was too stressed or tired. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed the bigger assignments that scared me, even when I knew starting early would help. The fear of not doing them well enough made me avoid them, which is ridiculous because avoiding them just made things worse. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but really I was just scared of failing. The gamified breakdown of tasks helped somewhat with this.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be relatively calm until about a week before something was due, then I'd be on the LMS constantly, frantically checking requirements and submitting work. The system would probably show these spikes of activity that look inconsistent, but really it was just me responding to pressure. I wish I could have been more consistent, but fear of deadlines was my main motivator.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it gave me a boost and made me want to engage more with similar activities. But when feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, I'd sometimes avoid that subject for a while because it felt discouraging. The gamified achievements provided more immediate feedback which helped keep me going even when assignment feedback took a while to come back.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my time, which I appreciated. Having the gamified elements gave me smaller goals to work toward, which made the whole thing feel less overwhelming. But with 20 courses, sometimes the structures conflicted with each other and I'd have to make tough choices about what to prioritize. More flexibility would have helped during really stressful periods.
Q19: The visual elements in the course materials made engagement much easier for me since that matches how I learn. The gamified tasks were great for breaking things into manageable pieces. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when I couldn't easily find resources I needed. Also, discussion forums felt awkward sometimes because I wasn't sure if my contributions were good enough.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or looking for resources without actually completing work yet. They could be scared like me and spending time preparing mentally before actually doing assignments. Or maybe they're struggling to understand the material and keep returning hoping it'll click. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: This definitely reflects part of my experience. I'd often do one focused session online to gather materials, then spend much more time studying offline where it wouldn't be tracked. Some people are just efficient learners who don't need to repeatedly access things online. Or they might have good study groups or external resources that help them succeed without heavy LMS use.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you engage, and what resources you access. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't capture whether you actually understood something or just clicked through it. They also miss the emotional experience, like how scared or stressed you felt while doing the work, which really affects learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get them done. It's when you're curious and want to learn more, when you think about course concepts even outside of study time. For me, real engagement is when I'm making connections between ideas and creating visual representations that help me truly grasp something. It's mental presence, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much fear and stress influenced my learning journey, or how hard I worked to overcome low motivation. It can't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams, or the study sessions with friends that really helped concepts click. It also misses how the gamified elements actually helped my mental state by making progress feel achievable. My engagement was deeper than my login frequency suggests.
